question: The image and the caption point to the same biology concept. Which topic best explains both pieces of evidence? Clue: Vectorised version of File:Three this topic growth types.png . Three types of this topic reproduction are compared: the relatively simple Binary fission and two more complicated types that either involve mitosis or meiosis . Binary fission . Organisms such as bacteria typically have a single chromosome (green). At the start of the binary fission process, the DNA molecule of the this topic's chromosome is replicated , producing two copies of the chromosome. A key aspect of bacterial this topic reproduction is making sure that each daughter this topic gets a copy of the chromosome. Cytokinesis is the actual physical separation of the two new daughter this topic. this topic reproduction that involves mitosis . Most eukaryotic organisms like humans have more than one chromosome. In order to make sure that a copy of each chromosome gets segregated into each daughter this topic, the spindle apparatus is used (blue threads). The chromosomes are moved along the long thin microtubules like trains moving along train tracks. Humans are diploid ; we have two copies of each type of chromosome, one from the father (red) and one from the mother (green). this topic reproduction that involves meiosis . The human sex this topic ( gametes ) are produced by meiosis. For sperm production there are two cytokinesis steps that produce a total of four this topic, each with half the normal number of chromosomes. The situation is different in the ovaries for egg production where one of the four sets of chromosomes that is segregated is placed in a large egg this topic, ready to be combined with the DNA from a sperm this topic (see meiosis for details). Note text is on a hidden layer under the visible text for consistent fonts.
caption: Vectorised version of File:Three cell growth types.png . Three types of cell reproduction are compared: the relatively simple Binary fission and two more complicated types that either involve mitosis or meiosis . Binary fission . Organisms such as bacteria typically have a single chromosome (green). At the start of the binary fission process, the DNA molecule of the cell's chromosome is replicated , producing two copies of the chromosome. A key aspect of bacterial cell reproduction is making sure that each daughter cell gets a copy of the chromosome. Cytokinesis is the actual physical separation of the two new daughter cells. Cell reproduction that involves mitosis . Most eukaryotic organisms like humans have more than one chromosome. In order to make sure that a copy of each chromosome gets segregated into each daughter cell, the spindle apparatus is used (blue threads). The chromosomes are moved along the long thin microtubules like trains moving along train tracks. Humans are diploid ; we have two copies of each type of chromosome, one from the father (red) and one from the mother (green). Cell reproduction that involves meiosis . The human sex cells ( gametes ) are produced by meiosis. For sperm production there are two cytokinesis steps that produce a total of four cells, each with half the normal number of chromosomes. The situation is different in the ovaries for egg production where one of the four sets of chromosomes that is segregated is placed in a large egg cell, ready to be combined with the DNA from a sperm cell (see meiosis for details). Note text is on a hidden layer under the visible text for consistent fonts.

question: If you had to sort this image onto one biology page using the caption as evidence, which topic would you choose? Clue: Map of the human mitochondrial DNA genome (16569 bp, NCBI sequence accession NC_012920 — Anderson et al. 1981 ). The H (heavy, outer circle) and L (light, inner circle) strands are given with their corresponding genes. There are 22 transfer RNA (TRN) genes for the following amino acids: F, V, L1 (codon UUA/G), I, Q, M, W, A, N, C, Y, S1 (UCN), D, K, G, R, H, S2 (AGC/U), L2 (CUN), E, T and P (white boxes). There are 2 ribosomal RNA (RRN) genes: S (small subunit, or 12S) and L (large subunit, or 16S) (blue boxes). There are 13 protein-coding genes: 7 for NADH dehydrogenase subunits (ND, yellow boxes), 3 for cytochrome c oxidase subunits (COX, orange boxes), 2 for ATPase subunits (ATP, red boxes), and one for cytochrome b (CYTB, coral box). Two gene overlaps are indicated (ATP8-ATP6, and ND4L-ND4, black boxes). The control region (CR) is the longest non-coding sequence (grey box). Its three hyper-variable regions are indicated (HV, green boxes).
caption: Map of the human mitochondrial DNA genome (16569 bp, NCBI sequence accession NC_012920 — Anderson et al. 1981 ). The H (heavy, outer circle) and L (light, inner circle) strands are given with their corresponding genes. There are 22 transfer RNA (TRN) genes for the following amino acids: F, V, L1 (codon UUA/G), I, Q, M, W, A, N, C, Y, S1 (UCN), D, K, G, R, H, S2 (AGC/U), L2 (CUN), E, T and P (white boxes). There are 2 ribosomal RNA (RRN) genes: S (small subunit, or 12S) and L (large subunit, or 16S) (blue boxes). There are 13 protein-coding genes: 7 for NADH dehydrogenase subunits (ND, yellow boxes), 3 for cytochrome c oxidase subunits (COX, orange boxes), 2 for ATPase subunits (ATP, red boxes), and one for cytochrome b (CYTB, coral box). Two gene overlaps are indicated (ATP8-ATP6, and ND4L-ND4, black boxes). The control region (CR) is the longest non-coding sequence (grey box). Its three hyper-variable regions are indicated (HV, green boxes).

question: Which Wikipedia biology answer can you justify only after reconciling the image with this redacted caption clue: A diagram of the Calvin cycle showing carbon capture from CO2 and carbon output as glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate. Carbon can also exit the cycle as glycerate 3-phosphate, for production of fatty acids, and some amino acids. What is shown here is only the canonical representation of the Calvin cycle, where carbon exits as glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate for gluconeogenesis, or glycerol production. Key: Circles represent atoms according to the following: Black is carbon . White is hydrogen . Red is oxygen . Purple is phosphorus . Alternative chemical names: 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA or 3PG) is also known as glycerate 3-phosphate or GP in British textbooks. Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate (G3P or GAP) is also known as triose phosphate or TP in British textbooks. Origin: This diagram is based on Calvin-cycle4.svg by Mike Jones , licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 Unported license.
caption: A diagram of the Calvin cycle showing carbon capture from CO2 and carbon output as glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate. Carbon can also exit the cycle as glycerate 3-phosphate, for production of fatty acids, and some amino acids. What is shown here is only the canonical representation of the Calvin cycle, where carbon exits as glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate for gluconeogenesis, or glycerol production. Key: Circles represent atoms according to the following: Black is carbon . White is hydrogen . Red is oxygen . Purple is phosphorus . Alternative chemical names: 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA or 3PG) is also known as glycerate 3-phosphate or GP in British textbooks. Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate (G3P or GAP) is also known as triose phosphate or TP in British textbooks.
